coredata.dc:title: Brightly blue triazine-doped carbon dots for selective determination of Cu(II) in environment and imaging in cell
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Man, Yun
Zou, Wen-Sheng
Kong, Wei-Li
Li, Weihua
Dong, Weiwei
Zhao, Donglin
Qu, Qishu
Wang, Yaqin
coredata.dc:description: Whether as a microelement or a highly toxic metal ion, it has a standing demand to the development of simple, facile and sensitive platform for copper ion (Cu(II)) assay. Here we reported the use of 2,4,6-trihydrazinyl-1,3,5-triazine (THT) and citric acid (CA) as precursors to fabricate carbon dots (C-dots) through one-spot hydrothermal approach at 200 °C for 10 h. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at ∼355 and ∼440 nm, respectively, of the triazine-doped C-dots with ∼27.6 % QY were observed. The functional groups such as −COOH and -NH2 anchored on the surface of the C-dots, which led to the pH-dependent emission behavior. Also, they can coordinate with Cu(II) to form an absorbent complex, quenching the blue fluorescence of the triazine-doped C-dots by inner filter effect, and the aggregation-induced quenching also contributed to the fluorescence decay. A chemosensory platform for Cu(II) with an 8.6 nM detection limit in a concentration ranging from 0.01–12.5 μM was therefore established. The triazine-doped C-dots were successfully applied in not only the determination in environment but also the imaging in cell of Cu(II), demonstrating their excellent applicability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triazine-doped carbon dots
Chemosensor
Cu(II) sensing
Cellular imaging
Inner filter effect (IFE)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis for Cu(II) of real-world water samples
Preparation and characterization of triazine-doped C-dots
Fluorescent responsiveness and selectivity to Cu(II) of triazine-doped C-dots
Possible response mechanism of triazine-doped C-dots to Cu(II)
Applications of triazine-doped C-dots in determination in environment and imaging in cell of Cu(II)
Figures merit of C-dots in real sample assay
Intracellular imaging of Cu(II) based on triazine-doped C-dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province ( 1908085MB40 ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Universities of Anhui Province ( KJ2018A0513 , KJ2019A0775 ) for financial support. The authors also gratefully acknowledge Doctoral scientific research foundation of Anhui Jianzhu University ( 2017QD15 , 2018QD60 ), and National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51978003 , 21777001 , 21876001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD pattern of the triazine-doped C-dots (A). The FTIT spectrum (B) and XPS spectrum (C) of the triazine-doped C-dots. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (D), N1s (E) and O1s (F).

Photoluminescent properties of the triazine-doped C-dots. Normalized UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of the triazine-doped C-dots (A). Inset is the photograph taken under 365 nm UV lamp. Fluorescence decay of the triazine-doped C-dots in aqueous solution (B).

The effects of Cu(II) on the fluorescence spectra of the triazine-doped C-dots aqueous solution (A). Insets are the corresponding plots of lg(F0/F) vs Cu(II) concentration within the range of 0.01–12.5 μM (Top) and the photographs of the triazine-doped C-dots aqueous solution before and after the addition of 12.5 μM Cu(II) under 365 nm UV light (Bottom). The selectivity of triazine-doped C-dots for Cu(II) assay over other ones in p H ∼7.4 (B). Arabic numerals from 1 to 15 represent Blank, NH4 +, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Fe3+ and Cu(II), respectively.

The UV–vis spectra of Cu(II) titrating triazine-doped C-dots aqueous solution (A). The comparisons of the UV–vis spectra of the absorbent complex with excitation and emission spectra of the triazine-doped C-dots (B).

Cellular imaging of Cu(II). (A-D) are washed Cells imaged under confocal ﬂuorescence of C-dots before (A) and after the addition of Cu(II) (B), bright ﬁeld (C), and their overlap (D), respectively. The scale bars are all 50 μm.

Schematic illustration of the preparation of triazine-doped C-dots (A), and the response of C-dots to Cu(II) (B), and intracellular imaging of C-dots (C) and Cu(II) imaging after the incubation of C-dots into HeLa cells.
Detected results of Cu(II) in various real-world samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Brightly blue triazine-doped carbon dots for selective determination of Cu(II) in environment and imaging in cell

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Bengbu University, 1866 Caoshan Road, Bengbu, Anhui, 233030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Bengbu University, 1866 Caoshan Road, Bengbu, Anhui 233030, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, and Anhui Provincial Laborary of Environmental Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, Anhui Jianzhu University, 292 Ziyun Road, Hefei, Anhui, 230022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, and Anhui Provincial Laborary of Environmental Pollution Control and Resource Reuse

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, and Anhui Provincial Laborary of Environmental Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, Anhui Jianzhu University, 292 Ziyun Road, Hefei, Anhui 230022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Whether as a microelement or a highly toxic metal ion, it has a standing demand to the development of simple, facile and sensitive platform for copper ion (Cu(II)) assay. Here we reported the use of 2,4,6-trihydrazinyl-1,3,5-triazine (THT) and citric acid (CA) as precursors to fabricate carbon dots (C-dots) through one-spot hydrothermal approach at 200 °C for 10 h. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at ∼355 and ∼440 nm, respectively, of the triazine-doped C-dots with ∼27.6 % QY were observed. The functional groups such as −COOH and -NH2 anchored on the surface of the C-dots, which led to the pH-dependent emission behavior. Also, they can coordinate with Cu(II) to form an absorbent complex, quenching the blue fluorescence of the triazine-doped C-dots by inner filter effect, and the aggregation-induced quenching also contributed to the fluorescence decay. A chemosensory platform for Cu(II) with an 8.6 nM detection limit in a concentration ranging from 0.01–12.5 μM was therefore established. The triazine-doped C-dots were successfully applied in not only the determination in environment but also the imaging in cell of Cu(II), demonstrating their excellent applicability.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion (Cu(II)) play a key and contradictory role in both environment and life. It is essential for varieties of living organisms. However, Cu(II) is not readily metabolized, and an excess of copper intake is highly toxic, and even low-dosage exposure to copper can cause kidney and liver damage, and even death [1]. Cu(II) is a non-renewable, non-biodegradable and potentially bio-accumulated [2], the United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) stipulates the Cu(II) contaminated industrial wastewaters can discharge to recipient waters via an adequate treatment of Cu(II) concentrations of effluents low to 1.3 mg/L [3,4]. Moreover, on the one hand, Cu(II) as a microelement, an adult needs an average daily Cu(II) dosage of 1.2 mg to keep a healthy body [5], which concerns with a large amount of physiological and pathological activities that involved in neurotransmitters and neuromodulators at different biological levels [6]. Cu(II), as a redox cofactor in a variety enzymes, takes part in not only the formation of nerve cells but also the activation process of various metalloenzymes [7]. On the other hand, long-term exposure to high-level Cu(II) or insufficient intake of Cu(II) can result in the generation of reactive oxygen species, activating the oxidative damage to neutral system, and inducing the neurodegenerative diseases [8–10]. Also, Cu(II) metabolism disorders and Cu(II) dyshomeostasis cause serious neurodegenerative diseases, which has been considered as one of the risk factors of neurological disorders [11,12]. In addition, the various accumulation in human body and diversity distribution in environment of Cu(II) exert a great difficulty in real time and on-the-spot monitoring. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop facile, rapid and sensitive approaches for monitoring of trace levels of Cu(II) in environmental and biological samples due to the gradually increasing needs for human health and green environment.

In recent years, many techniques for Cu(II) assay, such as inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [13,14], flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) [15], electrochemical sensors [16,17], wearable biosensors [18], fluorescence assay [19–21], and so on, have been reported. Comparatively speaking, electrochemical sensing owns a relatively poor reproducibility. ICP-AES and FAAS, etc., for the detection of Cu(II), as well as cost-expensive and time-consuming processes, require cumbersome sample pretreatment, making the on-the-spot and real-time monitoring of Cu(II) difficult, even impossible. Meanwhile, fluorometric methods are operation-simple, time-saving and even capable of revealing trace amounts of target molecules in vivo through functional fluorescent reporter that can “view” pathological events with high temporal-spatial resolution. In particular, with the nanometer technology springing up, carbon quantum dots (C-dots) as an emerging class of luminescent nanomaterials have drew considerable research interests and become a new study hotspot [22–25], which also contributed more to the detection of Cu(II) [26–30]. Among fluorometric assays, the organic dyes feature the bad photobleaching and poor water solubility besides the complicated separation and purification process [31]. The intrinsic high cytotoxicity of CdTe/Se QDs greatly limited their applications [32]. A low quantum yield and a hardly tuned excitation/emission perplexed the development of the Mn-doped ZnS QDs [33,34]. Similarly, the noble metal complexes and metal cluster favor a high cost and a poor stability, rendering them unavailable to apply to an aqueous environment. As for the advantages on C-dots, for instance, cheap raw materials, simple synthesis and purification process, tunable surface chemistry and multicolor emission, etc. [35], any one totally makes C-dots rise joint by joint like sesame flowers on the stem. Therefore, it is still highly demanded to develop cheap, highly sensitive and environment-friendly C-dots-based fluorescence probes for Cu(II) detection.

Herein triazine-doped C-dots were fabricated through a one-pot hydrothermal approach adopting citric acid (CA) and 2,4,6-trihydrazinyl-1,3,5-triazine (THT) as precursors at 200 °C for 10 h. The obtained triazine-doped C-dots can emit brightly blue fluorescence with quantum yield (QY) ∼27.6 %. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at ∼355 and ∼440 nm were observed. The –NH2 and –COOH groups anchored on the surface of the C-dots, endowing them electrolyte performance, and resulting in the pH-dependent emission behaviors. Moreover, the functional groups on the surface of the C-dots can complex with Cu(II) to form an absorbent complex, which, together with aggregation-induced quenching, quenched the fluorescence of the C-dots by an inner filter effect (IFE). A wide linear response to Cu(II) with a concentration ranging from 0.01–12.5 μM, and a detection limit of 8.6 nM and RSD of 4.8 % (n = 5) was therefore shown. Most significantly, the C-dots integrated low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, cheap cost, high QY and high photostability, successfully being applied to the confocal imaging of intracellular Cu(II).

The 2,4,6-trihydrazinyl-1,3,5-triazine (THT) with purity over 98 % was the product of Hangzhou Yuhao Chemical Reagents Ltd. (Hangzhou, China), and used as received. Citric acid (CA) was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All other reagents were purchased commercially and used without further purification.

Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR) was conducted on a Nicolet Nexus 670 spectrometer using KBr pellets (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA). The morphology size of the C-dots was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted with a PHI 5702 spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα exciting source. The fluorescence lifetime of C-dots was carried out in a time-correlated-single-photon-counting (TCSPC) system from FL920 P spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, U.K.) with excitation wavelength at 365 nm. All fluorescence spectra were an F-4600 spectrofluorophotometer equipped with a Xe lamp (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) using 5/5 nm slit width. The UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a Shimadzu 3100 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed on Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer by using Cu Kα (λ = 0.154056 Å) as the incident radiation. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed on Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer by using Cu Kα (λ = 0.154056 Å) as the incident radiation. The zeta-potential was analyzed by a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern, U.K.).

Briefly, 0.05 g of THT and 0.065 g of CA were dispersed in 50 mL double distilled water (DDW) under the assistance of ultrasonic for 30 min. The turbid solution was transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave, and then heated for 10 h at 200 °C. The cooled C-dots solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min for obtaining supernatant, which was then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MW = 10 000) for 48 h for further purifying the C-dots. During dialysis procedure, the water was exchanged every 3 h. The purified C-dots were partially preserved in refrigerator at 4 °C for further use. The rest were freeze-dried for subsequent characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis for Cu(II) of real-world water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real-world water samples from Yuwan wastewater treatment plant, Yihai lake in Anhui Jianzhu University, tap water were filtered through 0.45 μm Suporfilters. The mother liquors were adjusted pH to ∼7.4 prior to being collected in a precleaned glass bottle, which was stored in a refrigerator to further use. In order to assay for Cu(II) of real-world water samples, a certain amount of different real-world water samples and C-dots stock solution were transferred to a cuvette, and then Cu(II) was added with various amounts. The fluorescence intensities were measured one by one.

The cell viability assessment of the as-prepared C-dots was carried out using the MTT assay. Typically, 100 μL of cells was seeded in a 96-well plate with a density of 1 × 105 cells/well in Dulbecco’s Modiﬁed Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) medium for 24 h. In a humidiﬁed atmosphere with 5% CO2, 10 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL) was added to each well after the incubation with C-dots for 12 h at 37 °C. The media was removed after incubating for another 4 h at 37 °C, and 100 μL of DMSO was added to each well and shaken for 10 min. Finally, the absorbance was measured at 570 nm to assess the relative viabilities of the HeLa cells.

HeLa cells were seeded in a 12-well plate cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C in a humidiﬁed atmosphere of 5% CO2 for 12 h, followed by incubation with C-dots for 4 h, and then the cells were washed with fresh phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 10 mM, p H = 7.4) for three times to remove the residual C-dots. Finally, the cells were observed by a ﬂuorescence microscope equipped with a 20× objective. In order to assess Cu(II) uptake, exogenous Cu(II) was added to the cell culture medium and incubated for 0.5 h at 37 °C. The HeLa cells were washed with cold PBS for cell imaging.

Chemical methods, in particular, heteroatom doping can greatly better the photoelectric property of the C-dots [36]. The introduction of heteroatom into C-dots not only improves the characteristics through tuning the carbon skeleton matrices and chemical structure of the surface of C-dots [37], but also alters the electronic structures of C-dots via the formation of p-type and n-type carriers [38]. Moreover, the N,P co-doped C-dots owned brightly blue and green dual-emission under different excitation wavelengths [39,40], and N,S co-doped C-dots possessed an 73 % of high quantum yield [41]. Inspired by these achievements, we attempted to prepare N-containing C-dots, and expected the C-dots own an excellent photoluminescent performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of triazine-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1 A, the THT and CA were selected as raw materials to synthesize C-dots through a one-spot hydrothermal method. The amidation reaction was ﬁrstly occurred among amine of HTH molecules and carboxylic acid of CA ones to form poly-like composites, followed by being carbonized to form C-dots at high temperature. The TEM, Zeta-potential, XRD and EDS were conducted to study the morphology, surface charged performance, crystalline state and composition of the C-dots, respectively. The TEM image of the as-prepared C-dots reveals the C-dots were well-dispersed, the statistical particle size of C-dots was mainly distributed in the range of 1.5–4.5 nm with an average diameter of 3.2 nm (see Fig. S1). The Zeta-potential measured as −18.9 eV indicated the C-dots negatively charged (see Fig. S2). As depicted in Fig. 1 A, a typical XRD pattern of the C-dots shows a diffraction peak at 25.8° and a shoulder one at 41.5°, indicating 0.36 nm of the lattice parameter, and amorphous phase of C-dots due to the introduction of COC, COOR etc. groups [42,43]. The element analysis (EDS) of the selected area of the triazine-doped C-dots reveals the C-dots compose of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements. The weight percent (Wt%) and atomic percent (Atomic%) are 45.17 %, 10.60 %, 44.23 %, and 51.91 %, 10.39 %, 37.71 % for carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, respectively (see Fig. S3).

The functional groups on surface of the C-dots determine the application potentials. In this regard, the FTIR and XPS spectra were performed to identify functional groups on the surface of the C-dots. As shown in Fig. 2 B, FTIR spectrum exhibits a broad absorption band at around 3210–3415 cm−1, revealing the existence of NH2 and/or OH. Owing to the very dry FTIR sample, this absorption can most possibly attributed to NH2 and COH [44]. The peak located at 1129, 1408, 1577 and 1665 cm−1 corresponds to the CN, COO, CN and amide, respectively [45–47]. Among these, the absorptions at 1129 and 1577 cm−1 are considered as alternant CN and CN mode in triazine overring, respectively [48]. The absorption at 803 cm-1 can be assigned to the characteristic absorption of triazine overring, and the bands at 1070 cm−1 is regarded as the corresponding CNHC skeletal vibration [48]. These results, together with XRD pattern, indicate the triazine overring was intactly but unorderedly doped into the interior of the C-dots. XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 2C displays the C-dots mainly contain C (285.0 eV), N (399.5 eV) and O (531.0 eV) three elements, and the atomic percent are 52.26 %, 12.82 % and 34.91 %, respectively. This value is very near to that of element analysis (see Fig. S3). The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum can be devided into five peaks at 284.6, 284.9, 285.9, 287.4 and 288.0 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN, CO, CO and NCN bonds, respectively (see Fig. 1D) [47,49,50]. Also, the high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum contains five peaks at 398.2, 398.5, 399.2, 399.9, and 400.4 eV. Three peaks can be assigned to NH CO-NH (398.2 e V), NN (398.5 e V), NH (399.9 e V) [30,43]. The characteristic peaks at 399.2 and 400.4 eV correspond to CNC bond [47] and pyridine N (see Fig. 1E) [51,52]. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum can be deconvoluted into three types of oxygen bonds that can be assigned to C(O)OH (530.6 e V), COH (531.1 e V) and CONH (532.6 e V) (see Fig. 1F) [53]. FTIR and XPS results demonstrate that triazine overring was successfully doped into the C-dots, and numerous functional groups such as –COOH, –NH2 and –OH modified on the surface of the C-dots.

The photophysicochemical properties of the triazine-doped C-dots including UV–vis, maximal excitation and emission wavelengths, fluorescence lifetime and excitation-/pH-dependent emission were investigated. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the triazine-doped C-dots in aqueous solution shows that two typical UV–vis absorption bands. One centered at around 328 nm, the other was lower than 300 nm 285 nm (see Fig. 2A). The absorption bands below 300 nm and at around 328 nm may be attributed to the π–π* transition of CC bond and the n–π* transition of CO/CN bonds or pyridine N in C-dots, respectively [54]. In addition, it can be seen that the maximal excitation and emission wavelengths center at 350 and 440 nm, respectively. The relatively high QY of the C-dots was reflected by the photograph of the aqueous solution under 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 2A). Furthermore, using quinine sulfate as a reference, the QY of the as-synthesized triazine-doped was measured to be 27.6 % (Fig. S4A). As shown in Fig. 2B, the time-correlated single photon counting method was employed to measure the lifetime of the C-dots. The decay curve ﬁts well to a tri-exponent nature, and mean lifetime is calculated to be 3.6 ns.

To further explore the optical properties of the triazine-doped C-dots, the excitation-dependent emission behavior was carried out. With the excitation wavelength being increased from 300 to 350 nm, the emission accordingly enhanced. When the excitation wavelength was unceasingly increased from 360 to 400 nm, the emission reduced gradually. The emission profile kept unchanged at total wavelength range (see Fig. S5). Carbonization is a complicated chemical reaction process, the residual amine, carboxyl acids and hydroxy from raw materials partially shifted at the surface of the C-dots. In addition, it was reported water can participate the redox reaction to form carboxyl and hydroxy [55], etc., resulting in the performance of C-dots similar to ampholytes [43]. The pH-dependent emission behavior was also studied. The result displays the emission of the triazine-doped C-dots firstly enhanced at pH from 3.0–6.0, then gradually decreased at pH from 6.0–11.0. Meanwhile, a stable platform at pH from 6.0–9.0 was also took on (see Fig. S4B). The result exhibits the great potential of the triazine-doped C-dots in the field of cell imaging. For the simplification of the operation, the pH of aqueous solution was adjusted to 7.4 using PBS in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent responsiveness and selectivity to Cu(II) of triazine-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The –COOH, –NH2, etc., decorated on the surface of the triazine-doped C-dots, which can possibly coordinate with metal ions to form an absorbent complex [30,31,35,43,46]. Given the triazine-doped C-dots held a response to life ions or toxic metal ions, it will look for a promising approach for the C-dots to be an excellent sensing platform. Initially, we focus on Cu(II) mainly owing to Cu(II) as not only a microelement keeping a healthy body [5], but also a harmful factor activating the oxidative damage to neutral system [8–10]. The effect of Cu(II) on the fluorescence spectra of the triazine-doped C-dots was therefore explored. Prior to experiment, the relevant experimental conditions should be determined. 12.5 mg/mL of the triazine-doped C-dots showed a ∼900 a.u. of intensity under excitation/emission slits 5/5 and PMT 400 V. So this C-dots concentration was selected for all experimental conveniences. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when the Cu(II) was unceasingly added into the doped C-dots aqueous solution, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was quenched gradually. As the Cu(II) concentration was close to ∼20 μM, the quenching ratio of fluorescence was ∼75 %, and the doped C-dots aqueous solution turned dark (see bottom inset of Fig. 3A and Scheme 1B). The triazine-doped C-dots displayed a sensitive response to Cu(II), in that way, they will be utilized to quantitative assay for Cu(II). The plots of lg(F0/F) vs Cu(II) concentration showed a well linear correlation in concentration range from 0.01–12.5 μM (see top inset of Fig. 3A), and the equation Y = 0.04807C + 0.00265, R = 0.9987, was obtained by the linear fitting. Where Y is log(F0/F), F0 and F are the original and instant fluorescence intensity, respectively. C is the Cu(II) concentration in μM. The limit of detection (S/N = 3) was assessed to be 8.6 nM. 4.5 % of relative standard derivation (RSD) was calculated by 5.0 μM of Cu(II) detections (5 times), verifying the responsiveness and sensitivity of the triazine-doped C-dots for Cu(II) assay.

So far as a selective probe is concerned, the responsiveness and selectivity are the indispensible two factors. The coexisting ions in the real samples must be considerable for the determination of Cu(II). The anions such as Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3− and AC− (corresponding sodium salts) as interference were firstly took into account. The effects of various anions on the fluorescence intensity lower than 10 % were found, even the anions concentrations were high to 0.5 mM (data not shown). It can be seen from Fig. 3B, except for Fe(III), compared with ∼25 % of the retention ratio of fluorescence caused by 12.5 μM of Cu(II), higher than ∼80 % of that by other ions such as 0.5 mM of Na+, K+, NH4 +, Mg2+, Ca2+, and 20.0 μM of Zn2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, etc., was observed. As for Fe(III), it can be easily masked by SHPP [30,43] (see Fig. S6). These results indicate that the triazine-doped C-dots hold much better responsiveness to Cu(II) and much higher selectivity for Cu(II) over other metal ions, owning a tremendous potential as a fluorescence probe for the detection of Cu(II) in environment (see Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of triazine-doped C-dots to Cu(II)
ce:section.ce:para: Inner filter effect (IFE), as an important non-irradiation energy conversion model, lies in the absorption spectrum of the absorbent partially or totally overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum, or both spectra of the fluorophor, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of the fluorophor [56,57]. The matched donor-acceptor pair is the key factor for the construction of IFE chemosensors. In comparison with traditional techniques, the IFE-based ﬂuorescent approach is more ﬂexible, straightforward, and no need to modify donor and link donor and receptor [58]. The UV-via spectra of Cu(II) titrating the triazine-doped C-dots aqueous solution were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, when Cu(II) was unceasingly added into the triazine-doped C-dots aqueous solution, the gradual enhancement of absorption was observed, suggesting the formation of an absorbent complex [59–61]. The comparisons of the UV–vis spectra of the absorbent complex with the excitation and emission spectra of the triazine-doped C-dots were processed (see Fig. 4B). The main absorption of the absorbent complex overlapped with the excitation of the triazine-doped C-dots at wavelength range from 275 to 400 nm. So did the shoulder absorption of the absorbent complex with the emission of the triazine-doped C-dots at wavelength range from 400 to 500 nm, which meets the requirement of IFE to quench fluorescence. Experimentally, in the presence of increasing amounts of Cu(II), the fluorescence of the triazine-doped C-dots was gradually reduced (see Fig. 3A). In addition, when thiourea was added into the triazine-doped C-dots aqueous solution, the quenched fluorescence by Cu(II) was recovered, also testifying the interaction among C-dots and Cu(II) (see Fig. S7). Also, the coordination of metal ion with C-dots resulted in aggregation among inter-C-dots (see Fig. S8), giving rise to the fluorescence quenching [35]. The measurement of of Cu(II) concentration dependent lifetime gave the same result (see Fig. S9). Therefore, the quenching mechanism of the fluorescence of the triazine-doped C-dots by Cu(II) was well explained by both IFE and aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of triazine-doped C-dots in determination in environment and imaging in cell of Cu(II)
Figures merit of C-dots in real sample assay
ce:section.ce:para: For evaluation of the accuracy and reliability of present chemosensor under real conditions, the proposed sensing platform was conducted to detect Cu(II) in environmental water samples, for example, from Yuwan waste water treatment plant, Yihai lake in Anhui Jianzhu University and tap water of our lab, respectively. The samples via a simple pretreatment and adjustment of pH to ∼7.4 were analyzed by a standard-addition method by injecting certain amounts of Cu(II) into the real-world water samples. Prior to assay, the residual Cu(II) in water samples must be contrasted by ICP-AES method. Both the nearly changeless fluorescence intensities and no read-out result by ICP-AES revealed no Cu(II) in samples. The measured results well coincided with the spiked Cu(II) concentrations, and the quantitative recovery ratios ranged from 95.0%–105.3% (see Table 1 ), demonstrating the potential application of the triazine-doped C-dots probe for environmental monitoring of highly toxic Cu(II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular imaging of Cu(II) based on triazine-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Biocompatibility of the triazine-doped C-dots is very important for their further biological applications, especially in intracellular imaging, so the cytotoxicity of the triazine-doped C-dots was evaluated by MTT assay using HeLa cell lines. Under 12 h of the fixed incubation time, much higher concentration of C-dots led to the much lower cell viability (see Fig. S10). Most fortunately, more than 85 % of HeLa cells remained alive after the incubation with 60 mg/L triazine-doped C-dots for 12 h, indicating the cytotoxicity of the triazine-doped C-dots was low and owned the potential to further applied in cell imaging.

Subsequently, cell imaging experiments were carried out to further monitoring the availability of the as-prepared triazine-doped C-dots for imaging intracellular exogenous Cu(II) in living HeLa cells. Results shown in Fig. 5 exhibit a high brightness at cell membrane and cytoplasm of HeLa cells after the incubation of the Hela cells with C-dots for 4 h at 37 °C. In contrast, the interior of the cell nucleus looked very gloomy [31,42,43,62] (see Fig. 5A and D). After exogenous Cu(II) were incubated into the Hela cells pretreated by C-dots, almost no intracellular ﬂuorescence was observed as displayed by experiments in vitro (see Fig. 5B), testifying the triazine-doped C-dots can be applied to image intracellular Cu(II). The bright-ﬁeld transmission of Hela cells demonstrates clear cell images (see Fig. 5C). What is more, it did not observed an obvious morphological damage of the HeLa cells after the incubation with C-dots for several hours, further demonstrating our triazine-doped C-dots feature non-toxicity and well biocompatiblity, and can be served as an effective platform for intracellular Cu(II) imaging.

In summary, a chemosensory platform for Cu(II) was developed based on the triazine-doped C-dots using THT and CA as raw materials, which can detect Cu(II) low to 10 nM, successfully being applied in not only the determination in environment but also the imaging in cell of Cu(II). The limit of detection (0.55 μg/L) is much lower than those of the reported optical probes. Since both the US EPA and China (GB57459-2006) severely restricted the concentration of Cu(II) in drinking water to be less than 1.0 mg/L, this proposed method may be of potential usefulness in analyzing Cu(II) in water samples.

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1908085MB40), and the Natural Science Foundation of Universities of Anhui Province (KJ2018A0513, KJ2019A0775) for financial support. The authors also gratefully acknowledge Doctoral scientific research foundation of Anhui Jianzhu University (2017QD15, 2018QD60), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (51978003, 21777001, 21876001).

Yun Man: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Wen-Sheng Zou: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Wei-Li Kong: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Weihua Li: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Weiwei Dong: Conceptualization, Methodology. Donglin Zhao: Resources, Methodology. Qishu Qu: Writing - original draft. Yaqin Wang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing ﬁnancial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to inﬂuence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113321.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
